title: VCS hygiene — 90+ uncommitted files, 11 days since last commit; source of truth is an untracked working tree
what_failed: git log shows 24 commits total, last on 2026-05-18. Today is 2026-05-29. Meanwhile the working tree has 90 untracked + 12 modified files: closed tickets T-060..T-101, new ADRs (006, 007), new entrypoints (pi.py, pi_daemon.py), reports/, multiple new docs. Phase '8.5 complete, planning 8.7/8.8' exists only in loose files, not in history. The project's entire 'durable engineering biography' claim is undercut by the fact that 11 days of work lives only in an uncommitted working tree — one bad rm away from gone.
where_failed: git working tree; session_exit_protocol_checker.py does not warn on large uncommitted deltas
why_likely: PI.md rule 1 ('never git commit without explicit go') was meant to protect Ash's remote state, but it has no counterpart that flags when uncommitted work has piled up dangerously. The guardrail against surprise commits became an excuse for never committing.
migration_plan: 1. Decide what in the current 90-file delta is real and commit it (local branch — still honors 'no push without go').
2. Add a check to session_exit_protocol_checker.py: WARN when `git status --porcelain` exceeds N files (e.g. 25) or last commit is older than M days (e.g. 3).
3. Surface that warning in the session-end one-liner so it can't be ignored.
4. Clarify PI.md rule 1: 'never PUSH without go' stays absolute; local checkpoint commits on a working branch are encouraged, not forbidden.
5. Optionally: a daily local auto-commit to a wip/ branch via PiScheduler, never pushed.
escalation_2026_05_29: BIGGER than first written. .gitignore deliberately excludes the ENTIRE implementation — agent/, app/, core/, llm/, memory/, scripts/, testing/, tools/, pi_agent.py, evolution.py, requirements.txt — under '# Implementation — private, never publish'. git ls-files confirms 0 tracked files in agent/, testing/, tools/. So the actual source code and the entire test suite have NO version control anywhere — not private, not local. 'Don't publish' was conflated with 'don't track'. Consequence: zero history, no diffs, no blame, no rollback, no recovery if a core file is corrupted or a bad edit ships. The T-148 fix this session could not be committed for this reason. The git repo is a portfolio shell (docs+tickets+solutions) wrapped around an untracked codebase.
recommended_fix_for_vcs_gap: Option A (recommended): a SEPARATE PRIVATE git repo (or private remote) for the implementation — full history, never pushed to the public mirror. Keeps the 'never publish' rule AND gives the code real version control.
Option B: keep one repo but split remotes — public remote gets the portfolio paths, a private remote gets everything. Branch/worktree hygiene required.
Option C (minimum): a local-only git repo for the implementation dirs (init in a sibling path or use a second .git via GIT_DIR) so at least history exists on disk, even if never remoted.
Whichever: verify .gitignore still keeps implementation OUT of the PUBLIC repo. The goal is 'tracked privately', not 'tracked publicly'.
implementation_notes: This is process, not code, but it's the one that most directly contradicts the project's self-image. 'Reversibility' (PI.md preference 2: never delete, always archive) is meaningless if the archive itself is never committed — and the CODE is never committed anywhere at all.
analysis: PERSPECTIVE: The whole project is built on the idea of a durable, reversible record — tickets, solutions, lessons, ADRs. And right now that record exists only as untracked files in one working directory. That's not a nitpick; it's the project contradicting its own founding principle. Commit early, commit often, to a local branch — you already have the 'never push without go' rule to protect remote state, so local commits cost you nothing and protect everything. The session-check warning makes it automatic so discipline doesn't depend on remembering.
